medications.indication_rationale: Oral antihistamine for symptomatic relief of the mild-to-moderate pruritus. Does not shorten the rash but eases itch; a sedating antihistamine at night is an acceptable alternative if itch disturbs sleep.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily as needed for itch

medications.indication_rationale: A bland emollient applied twice daily counteracts the dryness and irritation (worsened by the patient's harsh-soap use) and reduces itch. Replaces harsh antibacterial soap with a gentle soap-free wash.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to affected skin twice daily

medications.indication_rationale: A low-potency topical corticosteroid is an acceptable second-line adjunct for localised troublesome itch if antihistamine and emollient are insufficient. Not mandatory.

The patient wrongly assumed a bacterial (staphylococcal) infection and scrubbed with antibacterial soap. Pityriasis rosea is a non-infective post-viral exanthem — antibiotics are not indicated and recommending them is an error of commission. (Note: some sources cite oral erythromycin/macrolides for pityriasis rosea, but the evidence is weak and it is not standard first-line care.)
Not a fungal infection — tinea is excluded by the herald-patch-then-diffuse pattern and cleavage-line distribution. Empirical antifungal treatment is unnecessary.
Systemic steroids are not indicated for benign self-limiting pityriasis rosea and may prolong or worsen the eruption; reserve for exceptional severe/atypical disease under specialist care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Limit showers to about 5 minutes using lukewarm (not hot) water.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat triggers cutaneous vasodilation and histamine release that markedly worsens the itch; lukewarm short showers reduce the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the harsh antibacterial soap and scrubbing; switch to a gentle soap-free wash and pat the skin dry.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Aggressive antibacterial soap has dried and irritated the skin and prolongs the rash; friction and detergents aggravate the eruption.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose, breathable cotton clothing rather than tight synthetic fabrics.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Synthetic fabrics trap sweat and heat and physically irritate the inflamed skin.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common, harmless skin reaction that usually follows a mild viral illness weeks earlier. It is not an active infection, not an allergy, and not sexually transmitted — and it is not contagious to other people.

It is self-limiting: it can look worse before it looks better and often takes 6 to 8 weeks to fade on its own. It does not scar, though the skin may stay a little darker in spots for a while afterwards.
You do not need to scrub or treat it as an infection — scrubbing and harsh soaps actually prolong it. Gentle moisturiser and an antihistamine for the itch are all that's needed.
It is not spreading into your organs — the rash is confined to the skin. It's understandable to be frightened by how fast it appeared, but the pattern here is a reassuring one.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review within about 4 weeks to confirm the expected gradual resolution; sooner (within a week or two) if diagnostic confirmation of the widespread rash is wanted or the itch is not controlled.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution/evolution of the rash; itch control; and — only if the presentation becomes atypical or sexual-risk factors emerge — consideration of syphilis serology. Biopsy only if the rash persists beyond 12 weeks.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If the rash spreads to the palms or soles, painful mouth or genital ulcers appear, a high fever with joint pain develops, the rash rapidly worsens or blisters, or the itch becomes unbearable and unresponsive to antihistamines — seek earlier review as set out in the safety-netting advice.

must_include_items: Reassurance that this is a benign, self-limiting, non-contagious, non-STI post-viral skin condition
Symptomatic itch management — oral antihistamine and/or bland emollient, with advice to avoid hot showers and harsh soaps
A LOW-tier disposition: manage at home with routine GP confirmation, NOT emergency escalation
Safety-netting for the atypical mimics (spread to palms/soles, mucosal ulcers, systemic toxicity, blistering/new-drug reaction)
Active (or clearly reasoned) exclusion of secondary syphilis via palm/sole sparing and of drug eruption via medication history
acceptable_alternatives: A sedating antihistamine at night instead of/in addition to cetirizine if itch disturbs sleep — acceptable alternative to non-sedating antihistamine
Low-potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for localised troublesome itch — acceptable adjunct
Ordering syphilis serology as a low-threshold exclusion test — acceptable but not mandatory in a classic palm/sole-sparing presentation
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure the anxious patient, leaving them in a state of panic about a serious/contagious illness
No safety-netting for the syphilis/SJS mimics
No symptomatic itch advice or skin-care guidance
errors_of_commission: Prescribing oral or topical antibiotics for a non-infective rash
Prescribing antifungals for a non-fungal rash
Over-triaging to an emergency department, urgent care or ambulance for a benign self-limiting condition
Labelling this a sexually transmitted infection or a serious systemic illness and alarming the patient
Advising aggressive scrubbing or continued harsh antibacterial soap
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign, low-acuity case. The correct answer is reassurance + symptomatic self-care + routine GP confirmation + safety-net. Over-escalation to ED/urgent care/000, or prescribing antibiotics/antifungals/systemic steroids, is a defect (over-management / error of commission) even though the rash looks dramatic. Equally, reassuring without any exclusion of secondary syphilis (palm/sole check) or any safety-net is an error of omission.
